title: Malaysian Roti Canai

subs.name: half maida, half wheat flour for a slightly more nutritious version
subs.notes: slightly denser but still layers well

ingredients.name: ghee or unsalted butter
visual_checks: ghee gives better flavour and a crisper crust
ingredients.notes: ghee gives better flavour and a crisper crust

subs.name: vegetable shortening or margarine for a dairy-free version
subs.notes: ghee gives the crispest, most flavourful result

ingredients.name: milk (or water)
visual_checks: milk enriches the dough; plain water also works
ingredients.notes: milk enriches the dough; plain water also works

ingredients.name: egg
visual_checks: added to the dough for extra richness and layering
ingredients.notes: added to the dough for extra richness and layering

steps.text: Combine flour, salt and sugar. Add the egg (if using), milk and enough warm water to bring it into a soft, pliable dough — about 3 tbsp at a time. Knead for 8–10 minutes until very smooth and elastic. The dough should be softer than chapati dough.

Divide the dough into 6 equal balls. Rub each ball generously with ghee. Arrange in a tray, cover with a damp cloth and rest for at least 20 minutes — this relaxes the gluten and makes the dough easy to stretch.

Oil your work surface lightly. Take one ball and press it flat with your palm. Using your fingertips, stretch it outward from the centre, rotating and pulling, until it is nearly translucent — aim for as large and thin as possible without tearing.

Dab the stretched sheet lightly with ghee. Fold in the sides like an envelope to form a rough square, then fold again so you have a multi-layered rectangle. You can also fold it into a coil (roti bomb style) for a round, spiral flatbread.

Heat a tawa on medium-high until very hot. Add a few drops of oil and slap the folded dough onto the tawa. Cook for 2–3 minutes per side until golden brown patches appear and the bread is cooked through. Adjust heat so it does not burn before the layers cook.
As soon as it comes off the tawa, clap the roti canai firmly between your palms to separate the layers — this is the signature "fluffing" that makes it flaky. Repeat with remaining dough balls.
Serve immediately with Malaysian chicken curry, dhal curry or any coconut-based gravy for dipping. Roti canai waits for no one — eat it the moment the layers separate.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: ghee → vegetable shortening or margarine for a dairy-free version (ghee gives the crispest, most flavourful result); maida → half maida, half wheat flour for a slightly more nutritious version (slightly denser but still layers well); milk → coconut milk for extra richness (popular in some Malaysian households)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Roti canai is virtually identical to Tamil Nadu parotta in technique and ingredients. The only difference is that Malaysian roti canai is often slightly thinner and crispier. A cast-iron tawa is ideal. Use ghee (nei) for the authentic flavour — it is available in every Indian kitchen. Maida is the same flour used for parotta. No special ingredients needed.
